Final Exam: December 9th (Friday), 9:45 am–12:00 pm   Clear, concise title Compelling visual design

Clear y-axis meaning

Clear x-axis meaning Outline • Operating Files

• Cloud Computing Why Use Files? • Until now, our programs have involved short, simple input, i.e., user types input via the keyboard. ⎻ What if we want to read a local/online file containing a dataset?

• Output has also been simple, with results displayed in the console, e.g., print(). ⎻ What if we want to write processed data into a file? What is File I/O? • "I/O" stands for “input/output” • Input: read the information in from a file on computer or online 1. Open the file 2. Read its contents into computer memory (RAM) 3. Close file • You can write information into a file. 1. Open an existing file (if it exists) / Create a new file 2. Write data into the file 3. Close file • Python has built-in functions that make this easy open() Function (1/2) • To read the contents of a file, we must tell Python ⎻ which file we will work with ⎻ what we will do with the file • open() returns a file handler, ⎻ in this example, the handler is assigned to a variable myFile

• Similar to “File -> Open” in MS Word open() Function (2/2) • One of the key functions for working with files in Python • To operate on files in Python, we need to perform operations below: ⎻ Open the file and associate the file with a file handler ⎻ Manipulate the file handler (e.g., read from or write to the file handler) ⎻ Close the file • The most important parameters are FILE_NAME, and MODE Parameter: FILE_NAME

• The argument to be assigned to this parameter is a string with the file name ⎻ "file1.csv" ⎻ "file2.dat" ⎻ "file3.txt" Parameter: MODE (1/2)

• This parameter is optional with "r" as the default • The argument for this parameter is a string that determines which of the modes the file is to be opened in ⎻ "r" (open for reading) ⎻ "w" (open for writing) ⎻ "a" (open for appending) Parameter: MODE (2/2)

Mode Description Starting position Creates file if not Wipes out exists existing file "r" Read-only Beginning of the file No No

"w" Write-only Beginning of the file Yes Yes (overwrite or create) "a" Append-only End of the file Yes No (append or create) Other Parameters

• . . . means there are other parameters for open(), e.g., ⎻ BUFFERING (optional) ⎻ ENCODING (optional) ⎻ ERRORS (optional) ⎻… • We will not use these parameters much in this course

Source: https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#open Examples of open() myFile1 = open("TutorHours.txt") # "r" implied myFile2 = open("abc.txt", "w") File Handler’s Attributes • Useful file handle attributes • Note: these are not methods (no parentheses needed)

Attribute Description file.closed Returns True if file is closed, False otherwise.

file.mode Returns access mode with which file was opened. file.name Returns the name of the file. Example of Attributes myfile = open("TutorHours.txt", "r") print("Name of the file: ", myfile.name) print("Closed or not : ", myfile.closed) print("Opening mode : ", myfile.mode)

Read Files myFile = open("TutorHours.txt")

• This line of code does the following: ⎻ Opens the file “TutorHours.txt” ⎻ …in “reading” mode by default ⎻ Assigns the opened file to the variable myFile ⎻ …myFile is the file handler • Once file is open, we can start reading it Three Common Ways to Read a File

1. Read the whole file in as one big long string myFile.read()

2. Read the file in one line at a time myFile.readline()

3. Read the file in as a list of strings (each element in the list contains one line in the file) myFile.readlines() (1) Read whole file into one string wholeData = file.read() wholeData

Literally one long string!

Escape sequence (\n) is TutorHours.txt printed, indicating a start on z111 Emily 1.5 4.5 6 5 a new line z211 Green 4.0 12 6.5 2.5 12 z359 Javad 6.0 4.0 3.0 8.0 2.5 (2) Read the file one line at a time lineOne = file.readline() lineOne

lineTwo = file.readline() lineTwo

TutorHours.txt z111 Emily 1.5 4.5 6 5 z211 Green 4.0 12 6.5 2.5 12 z359 Javad 6.0 4.0 3.0 8.0 2.5 (3) Read file as a list of strings listOfLines = file.readlines() listOfLines

TutorHours.txt z111 Emily 1.5 4.5 6 5 z211 Green 4.0 12 6.5 2.5 12 z359 Javad 6.0 4.0 3.0 8.0 2.5 Using for Loop to Read a File • Review: for loops useful to iterate through a collection of data ⎻ With a list, a for loop iterates over each element of the list (in order) • for item in myList… ⎻ Using range(), a for loop iterates over each number generated by the range (in order) • for n in range(15)…

• With a file, a for loop iterates over each line of the file (in order) Using for Loop and print() for line in file: print(line)

• for loop can iterate through the content of a file handler, line by line • An alternative way other than readline(), readlines()

Three empty lines, because each strings ends with '\n' and print() adds its own newline

Write to an Existing File • If we open myFile.txt with 'a' mode, writing to the file appends content at the end: f = open("myFile.txt", "a") f.write("Now file has MORE content!") f.close() • If we open myFile.txt with 'w' mode, writing to the file wipes out existing content f = open("myFile.txt", "w") f.write("File has started with NEW content!") f.close()

close() Method file.close() • It flushes any unwritten information and closes the file handle, after which no more writing can be done • Good practice to close a file to prevent corrupting the file and release the memory (RAM) What do we do with the whitespaces? • Whitespace is any “blank” character, that represents a space in a text but does not produce a visible mark • E.g., tabs, newlines, and spaces "\t" "\n" " "

• When we read in a file, we can read in all the whitespaces. But sometimes we don’t want to keep it strip()method It removes all whitespace from the start and end of a string stringRaw = "\t see s\n" stringClean = stringRaw.strip() stringClean Revisit Week 2 for more functions for string operations

strip() does not remove “interior” spacing

\t s e e s \n stringRaw Exercise Write a program that goes through all the lines in a file and reports the longest line to a file called “Output.txt” NLP.txt Natural language processing has its roots in the 1950s. Already in 1950, Alan Turing published an article titled "Computing Machinery and Intelligence," which proposed what is now called the "Turing test." The proposed test includes a task that involves the automated generation of natural language.

Output.txt Longest line = 150 Already in 1950, Alan Turing published an article titled "Computing Machinery and Intelligence," which proposed what is now called the "Turing test."  Solution 32

def identify_longest_line(fileName, outputFileName): content = open(fileName, encoding = "utf8") longest_line = "" for current_line in content: if len(current_line) > len(longest_line): longest_line = current_line content.close()

oFile = open(outputFileName, "w", encoding = "utf8") oFile.write("Longest line = " + str(len(longest_line)) + "\n") oFile.write(longest_line) oFile.close() #return

fileName = "NLP.txt" outputFileName = "output.txt" identify_longest_line(fileName, outputFileName) Solution (Alternative) def identify_longest_line(fileName, outputFileName): with open(fileName, encoding = "utf8") as content: longest_line = "" for current_line in content: if len(current_line) > len(longest_line): longest_line = current_line

with open(outputFileName, "w", encoding = "utf8") as oFile: oFile.write("Longest line = " + str(len(longest_line)) + "\n") oFile.write(longest_line) #return Unlike open() where you have to fileName = "NLP.txt" close the file with the close(), outputFileName = "output.txt" the with statement closes the file identify_longest_line(fileName, outputFileName) for you without you telling it to.

Read an Online File • We can read the online file directly into a Python variable from urllib.request import urlopen textpage = urlopen("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bbejeck/hadoopalgorithms/master/src/shakespeare.txt") text_all = str(textpage.read(), 'utf-8').replace("\n", " ") print(text_all[:100])

• Function urlopen is imported from module urllib.request • The urllib.request module defines functions to open URLs • Ed will allow the code above (security). Other platforms will (Google Colab and Jupyter Notebook).

Outline • Operating Files

• Cloud Computing  Traditional On-premises Data Storage High cost to set up and operate: • Upfront/initial capital costs of purchasing and maintaining the hardware • Ongoing operational costs of running and managing the infrastructure • Investment in additional infrastructure such as power, cooling, and physical security, as well as specialized staff to manage it

Typically, this is more expensive than cloud-based storage solutions!  We need to understand how to "talk" to the cloud and fetch data Large companies are shifting towards cloud services, as Cloud Computing and data analysis have become prevalent, e.g., Google Colab (short for Google Colaboratory) • Cloud-based platform provided by Google • Jupyter Notebook environment that allows users to write and execute Python code directly in web browsers • Hosted on Google's cloud infrastructure • Offers free access to computing resources, including CPU and GPU, for running code and performing data analysis tasks  Why have Cloud Computing and data analysis become prevalent? (1/2) • Scalability ‐ We can scale the computing resources up or down as needed, without the need for significant upfront investment in hardware ‐ This makes it easier for organizations to handle large amounts of data and handle sudden peak in traffic or usage • Cost-effectiveness ‐ We only pay for the resources we use, rather than investing in expensive infrastructure ‐ This allows organizations to save money on IT costs • Accessibility ‐ Cloud solutions provides access to data and applications from anywhere anytime ‐ This allows employees to work remotely, collaborate with others, and share data across different departments and locations  Why have Cloud Computing and data analysis become prevalent? (2/2) • Data Analysis ‐ We see an increasing amount of data being generated in organizations ‐ Cloud solutions allow us to process these large data sets and provide insights in real-time • Machine Learning & AI ‐ Cloud-based services provide the necessary computational power and infrastructure to perform complex machine learning and AI tasks ‐ This enables organizations to build and deploy models faster, and make better decisions based on the insights generated  Popular Cloud Platforms • Amazon Web Services (AWS) ‐ Comprehensive cloud platform. Provides a wide range of services, including compute, storage, databases, analytics, machine learning, and more ‐ Most widely used cloud platform, with large ecosystem of partners, third-party tools • Microsoft Azure ‐ Popular among organizations that already use Microsoft products and technologies ‐ Strong focus on AI and machine learning services • Google Cloud Platform (GCP) ‐ Popular among organizations that already use Google products and technologies ‐ Strong focus on AI and machine learning services Questions
